routes.rope_note: Single 60m rope for glacier travel plus short rappels off the ridge crest; trip report recommends carrying extra cord to leave at rappel anchors in case existing tat looks bad.
routes.ascender: Tibloc/prusik recommended for crevasse self-rescue given remote, low-traffic terrain
routes.corrections: Mountain Project route-specific trip report gives exact rack (2 pitons, few nuts, 4 cams); one short ice step was protected with rock gear, not screws. Curtis Ridge is described as harder/more committing than Liberty or Ptarmigan Ridge.

routes.rope_type: single (or twin 30m glacier ropes for 3-person teams)
routes.rope_note: 30m glacier/rando rope standard for a 2-person crevasse-rescue team; some parties instead run two 30m glacier ropes at 3 climbers/rope. Lightweight parties report 60m 8mm ropes.
routes.corrections: No rock rack — this is a snow/ice couloir (30-45 deg) with minimal crevasse danger through the finger itself; gear need is glacier crevasse-rescue kit only, not rock protection.

routes.rope_note: 30m glacier line typically adequate for this shorter, moderate route.
routes.corrections: No technical rock rack; protection is pickets (1-2 per person) with occasional ice screws depending on snow/ice coverage. Route is only advisable in winter/spring due to extreme rock/icefall in summer.

routes.rope_note: 60m rope enables two double-rope (30m) rappels off the ice headwall plus some downclimbing to regain the base of the ice pitches.
routes.corrections: No rock rack needed — 300ft AI2 ice headwall (~50 deg max), climbable in two pitches; gear is ice screws + pickets + a second ice tool. Primary hazard is icefall from the Kautz Ice Cliff seracs above.

routes.rope_note: Single 60m rope for glacier approach and ice-step pitches; the Grade IV right-hand variation adds 4-6 mixed pitches to AI2-3 on the way to the Liberty Cap Glacier.

sling_rack.note: highly broken volcanic rock — pitons/Tricams/cams hold better than nuts per multiple sources

routes.ascender: recommended for crevasse self-rescue on the approach glacier
routes.corrections: Rock gear composition differs meaningfully from granite Cascades routes — nuts flagged as unreliable in this rock type. Rockfall hazard is significant; sun-exposure timing matters more than gear here.

routes.rope_note: The 1948 first ascent used a single ~120ft (37m) rope with ice-axe and piton belays; a modern party would carry a 50-60m single rope. Extremely thin modern beta.

sling_rack.pitons: a few, for belay anchors in serac terrain
sling_rack.note: no dedicated rock rack found in any source — anchors are primarily ice-axe belays and piton belays across the icefall

routes.ascender: recommended — serac/crevasse-maze terrain with essentially no modern traffic or nearby rescue
routes.corrections: Very sparse route-specific gear beta: only the 1948 AAC first-ascent account and a SummitPost photo page exist for this route specifically. The original survey team explicitly recommended against using it as a summit route due to icefall hazard, and it sees essentially no modern ascents. Figures extrapolated from that account plus standard practice on adjacent Kautz-icefall/Wilson-Headwall terrain.

routes.rope_note: Single 60m rope typical for this sustained 3-4 day route; length isn't fixed across sources but 60m matches the multi-pitch mixed terrain reported.

sling_rack.note: three independent trip reports converge: 'a rock rack was essential'

routes.ascender: recommended for crevasse self-rescue on remote upper-mountain terrain
routes.corrections: Strongest multi-source agreement of the 10 routes that a real rock rack — not just a token piton — is required, unlike moderate Rainier routes. Ice screw count (6) and picket count (1-4, strongly condition-dependent) also corroborated across independent reports.

routes.rope_note: 60m rope with long slings reported for the upper Mowich Face finish shared by this route; 4-6 ice screws recommended for that section.

sling_rack.note: 'long slings' specifically called out for anchor extension on the upper Mowich Face finish

routes.ascender: recommended given remote, low-traffic, strongly condition-dependent terrain
routes.corrections: Route shares its final section (upper Mowich Face) with the Mowich Face route, so gear needs converge there. Only reliable in good snow years — recent reports note dry seasons expose rock and increase difficulty.

routes.rope_note: Standard glacier rope; late-season parties specifically report needing to rappel into and ice-climb out of crevasses rather than just cross on snow bridges.
routes.ascender: mechanical ascender (Tibloc/Micro Traxion) or prusiks specifically valuable — sources note late-season parties may need to climb out of crevasses on this remote, low-traffic route
routes.corrections: No rock gear — pure glacier route, but notably more serious than its Grade III suggests because of crescentic upper crevasses requiring careful route-finding; crevasse-rescue technique must be 'nailed' before attempting given the isolation.

routes.rope_note: A direct trip report's minimalist rack used one rope (consistent with a 60m alpine rope); the route requires rappels off ice cliffs (e.g. ~75ft) using doubled strands off a single rope, plus an extra sling used at one rappel anchor.

sling_rack.note: verbatim from a direct trip report: 'a rope, a cam, a few nuts, three screws, and one piton' — deliberately minimalist for speed through icefall exposure

routes.ascender: recommended — most serious/committing of the 10 routes, extended icefall/rockfall exposure with essentially no rescue access
routes.corrections: Directly sourced trip-report gear list: 1 cam, a few nuts, 1 piton, 3 ice screws, 1 rope, 2 ice tools. Confirms the lightweight ethos typical of Willis Wall ascents.
